draft.introduction: Genesis 11:1–9 recounts the Tower of Babel, where humanity’s attempt to “make a name for ourselves” and reach heaven results in the confusion of languages and scattering of peoples. From the Septuagint.

translation.text: And all the earth was one lip and one voice for all. And it came to pass as they moved from the east, they found a plain in the land of Shinar and settled there. And they said to one another, “Come, let us make bricks.” And they said, “Come, let us build for ourselves a city and a tower whose top will be to heaven, and let us make for ourselves a name, lest we be scattered over the face of all the earth.”
translation.original_text: καὶ ἦν πᾶσα ἡ γῆ χεῖλος ἓν καὶ φωνὴ μία πᾶσιν

translation.text: And the Lord said, “Behold, the people is one and they all have one lip, and they have begun to do this, and now nothing will be held back from them of all that they have set out to do. Come, let us go down and confuse their language there, so that they will not understand each other’s voice.” And the Lord scattered them from there over the face of all the earth, and they ceased building the city.
translation.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν κύριος · ἰδοὺ γένος ἓν καὶ χεῖλος ἓν πάντων

translator_notes.note: The divine plural “let us go down” (καταβάντες ) mirrors the plural of Genesis 1:26 (“let us make man”). Early Christians read this as a Trinitarian hint. The Babel narrative was also understood as reversed at Pentecost (Acts 2), where the Spirit restores communication across languages.
